pages: Sylla waves you over, cradling a potted fern. 'This is Meridian. I named her after my sister.'
'Back home I catalogued plants that never answered. Here... watch.' The fern's fronds turn toward you, slowly, like a face.
'Every species on Veridia listens. I stopped feeling alone the day I noticed.'
Sylla is waiting for you with two cups of something warm and violet.
'I filed my last report to the company empty. Whatever this planet is becoming, they don't get to own it.'
'But you should know everything I know. Sit. This will take a while — and it starts under your farm.'
Hux jerks a thumb at a crate of dented drill heads. 'Every one of those broke on the same seam under your field.'
'Rock shouldn't hum, farmer. But I'll tell you what I told nobody: it hums in tune.'
He scuffs the dirt with his boot, not looking at you. 'Carried that around two years. Feels lighter, saying it to somebody who won't laugh. ...You won't. Right?'
Hux pulls out a battered recorder. 'Bore shaft twelve. Listen once, then we never speak of it.'
Through the static: a slow, enormous breathing. In... out. Patient as tides.
'I sealed that shaft,' Hux says quietly. 'Not because I was scared. Because it sounded asleep, and waking things early is rude.'
Tinks beckons you behind her shop, where the wreck of her ship rusts in the grass.
'Engine two died in orbit. I should be a crater. Instead something caught me — softly, like a hand under a falling leaf.'
'I sell junk because it's honest work. But I stay because I owe this rock a life.'
Tinks is sitting on the wreck's wing, watching the moons. She pats the metal beside her.
'Fixed the nav computer last month. Didn't tell anyone. I could leave, you know.'
'Then I watched you hauling water at dawn like a lunatic, and I thought: no. This is the first place that ever caught me. You don't fly away from that.'
CARE-7's chest panel opens. Inside, pressed under glass, is a single dried Whisper Wheat stalk.
'THE PREVIOUS FARMER GREW THIS. THIS UNIT KEPT IT WHEN THEY FLED. ORGANIC MEMORY REQUIRES MAINTENANCE.'
'YOU ARE THE SECOND FARMER. THIS UNIT WOULD LIKE TO KEEP SOMETHING OF YOURS SOMEDAY. NOT YET. SOMEDAY.'
CARE-7 plays a sealed log. A voice you don't know — the last farmer — shaking: 'It spoke my name. I'm done. I'm sorry.'
'THIS UNIT HAS ANALYZED THE RECORDING 11,204 TIMES,' CARE-7 says. 'CONCLUSION: THE VOICE BELOW WAS NOT THREATENING. IT WAS INTRODUCING ITSELF.'
'THE LAST FARMER RAN FROM A GREETING. THIS UNIT BELIEVES YOU WILL ANSWER IT.'
Juno drags you into the comms shack, headphones already on your ears. 'Listen. LISTEN.'
Static. Then, underneath: a chord, vast and slow, rising out of the ground itself.
'I recorded that under YOUR field,' she grins. 'Best signal on the planet. You're famous, farmer. To me, anyway.'
Juno's shack is papered with waveform printouts. She's circled one in red.
'Every time you harvest, the ground answers. Same three pulses. I ran it through every codec I know.'
'It's not noise. It's grammar. Three pulses: acknowledge, appreciate, anticipate. It's saying thank you — and it's waiting for something. I think it's waiting for you.'
